column39:
Protocol to amend the Framework Agreement on Comprehensive Economic Co-operation and certain Agreements thereunder between the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) and China.
The Protocol provides amendments to the text of the Rules of Origin (ROO) Chapter and introduces a new section on Customs Procedures and Trade Facilitation (CPTF) to the Trade in Goods Agreement. In addition, the Protocol includes a third package of specific commitments under the Agreement on Trade in Services and adds provisions on the promotion and facilitation of investment in the Investment Agreement. Lastly, the Protocol introduces additional rules and areas of coverage for Economic and Technical Cooperation and a Future Work Programme on the further liberalisation of trade in goods, review of product specific rules, and investment liberalisation and protection.

column39: The Second Agreement Concerning Amendment to the CEPA Agreement on Trade in Services eliminates the three-year substantive business operation requirement for most service sectors, providing Macao service providers with easier access to the Mainland market. Additionally, it introduces pilot initiatives in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area and includes expanded liberalization measures in sectors such as high-tech, financial services, and audio-visual culture. Moreover, it grants professionals in fields like construction and engineering, accounting, medical care, and education facilitative entry conditions into the Mainland of China, empowering Macao service providers to capitalize on their professional strengths and pursue business growth opportunities in the Mainland of China. The Second Amendment Agreement came into effect on the day of signing (i.e. 10 October 2024) and will be implemented on 1 March 2025.

column39:
Notification of the Second Agreement Concerning Amendment to the Agreement on Trade in Services of the Mainland and Hong Kong Closer Economic Partnership Arrangement.

The Second Agreement Concerning Amendment to the Agreement on Trade in Services of the Mainland and Hong Kong Closer Economic Partnership Arrangement (“the Amendment Agreement II”) updates the commitments on liberalisation and facilitation of trade in services under the Agreement on Trade in Services notified to the WTO on 23 March 2016 (WT/REG162/N/1/Add.9 and S/C/N/264/Add.9) and implemented on 1 June 2016 so as to further enhance the level of liberalisation on trade in services under the framework of CEPA.  The Agreement on Trade in Services was first amended on 21 November 2019 as notified to the WTO on 18 March 2020 (S/C/N/264/Add.11 and S/C/N/264/Add.11/Corr.1) to add liberalisation measures which have been implemented since 1 June 2020.  The Amendment Agreement II introduces new liberalisation measures in a number of services sectors, including measures for pilot implementation in the nine Pearl River Delta municipalities in the Guangdong-Hong Kong-Macao Greater Bay Area, and includes commitments on domestic regulation for trade in services and facilitation measures on trade and investment, etc.  The Amendment Agreement II came into effect on the day of signing (i.e. 9 October 2024) and will be implemented on 1 March 2025.

column39:
Notification of the entry into force of Decision No. 1 of 2025 of the EFTA-Jordan Joint Committee concerning amendments to Protocol B to the Free Trade Agreement between the EFTA States and the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan concerning the definition of the concept of "originating products" and methods of administrative cooperation, and its Annexes.

Taking into account the adoption on 7 December 2023 by the Joint Committee of the PEM Convention of a new set of modernised rules of origin designed to promote trade between the Contracting Parties to the PEM Convention, the EFTA States and Jordan have agreed to amend Protocol B to the FTA concerning the definition of the concept of "originating products" and methods of administrative cooperation, and its annexes to reflect the latest development and incorporate a dynamic link to the revised PEM Convention.

Key changes include greater flexibility in cumulation of origin, allowing products processed across multiple PEM Contracting Parties to retain their originating status. The new rules also permit duty drawback for most products. In addition, simplified and more flexible product-specific rules are introduced, together with a higher tolerance threshold for non-originating materials. Further flexibility is also provided for accounting segregation. Registered exporters will have the possibility, if agreed between the Parties, to replace certificates of origin with statements on origin, and the issuance of electronic origin certificates is allowed. These updates aim to reduce administrative burdens, simplify trade documentation, and offer businesses additional opportunities within the PEM zone.

To ensure full alignment, as the Bilateral Agreements on Agriculture between the respective EFTA States and Jordan contained a separate set of rules of origin, they were amended separately with a reference to Protocol B on the same date as the adoption of Joint Committee Decision No. 1 of 2025. These revisions ensure that the updated rules of origin and related procedures are consistently applied across the Free Trade Agreement and the Bilateral Agricultural Agreements, thereby maintaining coherence and facilitating smoother trade operations between the Parties.

column39:
Notification of the entry into force of Decision No. 1 of 2021 of the EFTA-North Macedonia Joint Committee concerning amendments to Protocol B concerning the definition of the concept of originating products and methods of administrative cooperation.

The revised rules of the Regional Convention on pan-Euro-Mediterranean preferential rules of origin (PEM Convention) introduce a new set of modernised rules of origin designed to promote trade between the Contracting Parties to the PEM Convention. The EFTA States and North Macedonia have agreed to amend and replace Protocol B to the Free Trade Agreement (FTA) concerning the definition of the concept of “originating products” and Methods of Administrative Cooperation, incorporating a dynamic link to the PEM Convention and applying the revised rules of the PEM Convention on a bilateral basis pending their adoption by the Joint Committee of the PEM Convention.

Key changes include greater flexibility in the cumulation of origin, introducing simplified, more flexible product-specific rules, as well as allowing for the issuance of electronic origin certificates. These updates aim to reduce administrative burdens, simplify trade documentation, and offer businesses more opportunities within the PEM zone.

As Protocol B are referenced in the Bilateral Agreements on Agriculture between the respective EFTA States and North Macedonia, the amendments also necessitated corresponding changes to the bilateral agricultural agreements. These revisions ensure that the updated rules of origin and related procedures are consistently applied across the FTA and the Bilateral Agricultural Agreements, maintaining coherence, and facilitating smoother trade operations between the Parties.

Notification of the entry into force of Decisions No. 1 – 3 of 2008 of the Joint EFTA-Palestinian Authority Committee concerning amendments to Protocol A concerning processed agricultural products, to Annex I concerning products not covered by the Agreement and to Annex II concerning fish and other marine products.

Decision 1/2008: Taking into account that the EFTA States have revised their agreements with the European Union on processed agricultural products, by expanding the product coverage of these agreements and providing for improved concessions for the products covered, the Decision updates Protocol A to ensure products originating in the West Bank and the Gaza Strip are accorded treatment no less favourable than that accorded to the European Communities. 

Decision 2/2008: Update of Annex I following amendments to Protocol A to the EFTA-Palestinian Authority Agreement given that some products listed in the former Annex I to the Agreement are now included in the Agreement. 

Decision 3/2008: Technical amendments to Table 1 of Annex II to the EFTA-Palestinian Authority Free Trade Agreement to harmonise the scope of the Annex, taking into consideration developments in international trade in fish and other marine products and in particular the discussions taking place in the WTO regarding the definition of fish and fisheries products.

column39:
Notification of the entry into force of the:

•	"Agreement to amend the Free Trade Agreement between the Government of Canada and the Government of the Republic of Chile, done at Santiago on 5 December 1996", the incorporation a chapter on Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures and a chapter on Technical Barriers to Trade, as well as amendments agreed upon in the Government Procurement chapter of the Agreement; and 

•	"Agreement to amend, in respect of investment and trade and gender, the Free Trade Agreement between the Government of Canada and the Government of the Republic of Chile, done at Santiago on 5 December 1996", concerning amendments agreed upon in the Investment chapter of the Agreement, as well as the incorporation of a chapter on Trade and Gender.

The first Agreement includes amendments to the existing government procurement chapter, as well as a new sanitary and phytosanitary measures chapter and a new technical barriers to trade chapter. 

The new sanitary and phytosanitary chapter provides scope for better communication and cooperation between Canada and Chile, including through the modernized bilateral Sanitary and phytosanitary Committee, to address issues that may impact trade in agricultural, fish and forestry products while maintaining the rights of Parties to take easures necessary for the protection of human, animal or plant life or health.

The new technical barriers to trade chapter builds on the World Trade Organization (WTO) TBT Agreement, which ensures that technical regulations, standards, and conformity assessment procedures such as product testing and certification do not create unnecessary obstacles to international trade. In particular, the chapter incorporates WTO TBT plus obligations that address transparency, conformity assessment procedures, joint cooperation, and the creation of a Technical Barriers to Trade Committee, to address bilateral TBT issues such as the implementation of the Chapter.

The amendments to the government procurement chapter allow for reduced time period for submitting tenders, when the tendering procedure is made by electronic means. This adjustment promotes the use of electronic platform - including by publishing notices and accepting tender documentation by electronic means - helping to speed up procurement processes and improve its overall efficiency.

The second Agreement includes amendments to the existing investment chapter and a new chapter on trade and gender. 

The new trade and gender chapter acknowledges the importance of applying a gender perspective to economic and trade issues. The chapter provides a framework for Parties to cooperate on issues related to trade and gender and commits both sides to the creation of a trade and gender committee that will, among other things, share knowledge and best practices of programs for women entrepreneurs and women led SMEs, and encourage women’s participation in national and international economies.

The amendments to the investment chapter introduce new, inclusive elements, including language that reaffirms a states' right to regulate in the public interest and a new, dedicated article on corporate social responsibility (CSR). The update also includes procedural enhancements to the investor-state dispute settlement mechanism.

In 2014, Canada and Israel agreed to modernize the Canada-Israel Free Trade Agreement (CIFTA), a goods-only agreement in force since January 1, 1997. Through two phases of negotiations in 2014-2015 and 2017-2018, updates were made to four existing chapters:

• market access for goods
• rules of origin
• dispute settlement
• institutional provisions.

Nine new chapters were added that deal with new and ongoing trade elements and issues:

• e-commerce
• intellectual property
• labour
• sanitary and phytosanitary measures
• technical barriers to trade
• the environment
• trade facilitation
• trade and gender
• small- and medium-sized enterprises

column39: The Treaty on European Union and the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union as well as the Treaty Establishing the European Atomic Energy Community ceased to apply to the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland pursuant to Article 50(3) of the Treaty on European Union. This happened with the date of effectiveness of 1 February 2020, but a transition period lasted until 31 December 2020. On this basis, the internal market of the European Union, which includes the free movement of goods and the free movement of services, no longer includes the United Kingdom after 31 December 2020. Northern Ireland and the Sovereign Base Areas in Cyprus, however, remain included in certain respects.

column39:
Three changes to the Association of the Overseas Countries and Territories (OCT) with the European Union (original notification L/3467 of 14 December 1970 and subsequent reports) have taken place in these last years.

First, the current legal basis of the Association of the Overseas Countries and Territories (OCTs) with the European Union is the European Council Decision 2013/755/EU on the association of the overseas countries and territories with the European Union (OJ L 344, 19.12.2013). It has replaced the Council Decision 2001/822/EC of 27 November 2001 on the association of the overseas countries and territories with the European Community (OJ L 314, 30.11.2001, p. 1) as of 1 January 2014.

Second, some changes occurred also in the territorial scope of this Association, as a consequence of the amendments to the list of the OCTs contained in Annex II of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU).

1. On 1 January 2014 Mayotte ceased to be an overseas country and became an outermost region of the Union within the meaning of Article 349 TFEU. Therefore, Mayotte is not anymore listed in Annex II of TFEU and it is not covered by the legal provisions of Council Decision 2013/755/EU applicable to OCTs. This means that Mayotte falls outside the territorial scope of this Association agreement and it is part of the EU internal market.

The legal basis of this amendment is the European Council Decision 2012/419/EU of 11 July 2012 amending the status of Mayotte with regard to the European Union (OJ L 204, 31.7.2012, p. 131)

2. On 1 January 2012 Saint-Bartélemy ceased to be an outermost region and became an overseas country listed in Annex II of the TFEU. Therefore, Saint-Bartélemy is now subject to the legal provisions of Council Decision 2013/755/EU. This means that Saint-Bartélemy is covered by this Association agreement and it is not anymore part of the EU internal market.

The legal basis of this amendment is the European Council Decision 2010/718/EU of 29 October 2010 amending the status with regard to the European Union of the island of Saint-Barthélemy (OJ L 325, 9.12.2010, p. 4.).

Third, on 1 January 2014, Bermuda started being covered by the provision of the Association Agreement, on the basis of the legal provisions of the European Council Decision 2013/755/EU. To be recalled, Bermuda was already listed as an OCT in Annex II of TFEU, but it was not covered by the 2001 EU-OCT decision.

column39:
Notification of the entry into force of Decision No. 2 of 2013 of the EFTA-Peru Joint Committee concerning amendments to Appendix 2 to Annex V concerning product-specific rules to bring them in line with HS2012.

Noting the needs of facilitating international trade and aiming at ensuring legal certainty and predictability for economic operators, the EFTA States and the Republic of Peru have amended origin rules in accordance with the Harmonized System to improve the effectiveness of this Agreement. This amendment provides an updated list of product-specific rules, specifying the working or processing required on non-originating materials for the product to acquire originating status.

column39:
The Free Trade Agreement between the Republic of Macedonia and Ukraine, concluded on 18 January 2001, is amended by signing two decisions:
Decision No. 1/2022 established by the Agreement on Free Trade, for replacing Protocol B to the Agreement, with new Protocol B and Annexes to it which are an integral part of this Decision. Protocol B applies to products referred to in Chapter II of the Agreement and provides further liberalization of trade in agricultural and food-processing products in import to both countries. New Protocol B covers separate Annex I (a), (b), (c) and Annex II (a), (b) and (c) for further abolishment of custom duties to zero applicable duty or within tariff rate quotas, partially or gradual reduction of customs duties, and all duties having equivalent effect. 
Decision No. 2/2022 established by the Agreement on Free Trade, for replacing Protocol C to the Agreement, with new Protocol C, where to the existing bilateral system of rules of origin is replaced with pan-Euro-Mediterranean model protocol on preferential rules of origin, with aim to allow diagonal cumulation among the countries of the pan-Euro-Mediterranean cumulation comprising North Macedonia and the participants in the European Union’s Stabilisation and Association process, and  the European Union,  EFTA States, Faroe Islands, the Republic of Türkiye, Republic of Moldova, Georgia and Ukraine.

column39:
Notification of the entry into force of Decision No.1 of 2021 of the EFTA-Albania Joint Committee concerning amendments to Protocol B concerning the definition of the concept of originating products and methods of administrative cooperation.

Taking into account that the EFTA States and the Republic of Albania have adopted the Regional Convention on Pan-Euro-Mediterranean Preferential Rules of Origin, the Decision updates Protocol B to align with the revised Convention's framework and its subsequent amendments. This incorporation ensures that products originating in the EFTA States and Albania benefit from the same rules of origin, including cumulation rules, as other participants to the Convention.

column39: The EFTA States and the Republic of Serbia agreed to implement transitional provisions pending the revision of the Regional Convention on Pan-Euro-Mediterranean preferential rules of origin. The Decision updates Protocol B to include alternative applicable rules of origin and provides detailed rules for issuing electronically issued movement certificates EUR.1. This ensures continued preferential treatment and cumulation benefits. Additionally, the Decision includes provisions for the prohibition of drawback of customs duties, dispute settlement mechanisms, and procedures for handling withdrawal from the Convention.

column39:
The Free Trade Agreement between Georgia and Türkiye was signed on 21 November 2007, and entered into force on 1 November 2008. 
Rules of origin of the Free Trade Agreement were regulated under Protocol II of the Agreement. 

•	The Decision (No 1/2016) of the Joint Committee established by the Free Trade Agreement between the Government of the Republic of Türkiye and Georgia replacing Protocol II to the Free Trade Agreement between the Republic of Türkiye and Georgia concerning the definition of the concept of "originating products" and methods of administrative cooperation signed on 28 October 2016 in Ankara. The Decision has entered into force on 29 April 2021; and is applicable as of this date. The Decision makes reference to the rules of origin contained in the Regional Convention of the Pan-Euro-Mediterranean Preferential Rules of Origin, and enables diagonal cumulation between Georgia, Türkiye and the European Union.

column39:
Notification of the entry into force of the Protocol Amending the Free Trade Agreement between the EFTA States and Montenegro.

Taking into account that the EFTA States and Montenegro have adopted the Regional Convention on Pan-Euro-Mediterranean Preferential Rules of Origin, this Protocol set out a new Annex VIII to their Free Trade Agreement, covering rules of origin and methods of cooperation. The updated Annex VIII ensures Parties apply the same revised rules of the Convention and its subsequent amendments assuring that products originating in the EFTA States and Montenegro adhere to the same rules of origin, including cumulation, as other Convention participants.

The Agreement covers trade in goods, including industrial products, fish and other marine products, and agricultural products. In addition, it includes provisions on trade in services, investment, intellectual property rights, government procurement, competition, trade and sustainable development, and cooperation, as well as institutional and dispute settlement provisions. The Agreement establishes a Joint Committee which, among other functions, will supervise and examine the application thereof.
The Agreement is foreseen to enter into force in 2014, pending the completion of internal procedures by the Parties.
